subject: english
chapter_title: The Case of the Fifth Word (Mystery and Magic)

main_sections.title: The Case of the Fifth Word
main_sections.prose_summary:
The story is adapted from Donald J. Sobol's Encyclopedia Brown series and centres on Leroy Brown, an exceptionally gifted eighth-grader nicknamed 'Encyclopedia' because of his vast, encyclopaedic knowledge. His father, Chief Brown, is the head of the Idaville Police Department, and their usual practice is for the father to share unsolved cases at the dinner table, where Leroy quietly solves them before the meal ends. The story opens on a Tuesday evening when Chief Brown is visibly troubled. He explains that Tim Nolan, a man long suspected of involvement in a million-dollar jewellery heist from the Diamond Mart five years earlier, has died of a stroke. During the robbery, one of two masked men—believed to be Nolan and his accomplice Daniel Davenport—had his mask slip, but the clerk was not certain enough to testify. No stolen jewellery was ever found, and Davenport disappeared after the crime. That morning, officers had searched Nolan's property—a small palm-tree nursery—and found nothing.

The twist lies in the document found clipped to Nolan's dying will: a sheet from a desk calendar bearing Davenport's name and address, and four cryptic words—'Nom Utes Sweden Hurts'. Chief Brown and Mrs. Brown try to decode the words independently. Mrs. Brown identifies their literal meanings (nominative, an American Indian tribe, a European country, and the verb hurts) but cannot piece together a hidden message. Leroy, however, silently analyses the code. He asks one perfectly targeted question: Is there a young fir tree in Nolan's nursery? When his father confirms there is one on the south side, Leroy reveals that each word is a day of the week with the letters 'd-a-y' removed—Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday—and the unwritten fifth word (the hiding place) is 'Fir', standing for Friday. The stolen jewellery is discovered inside a twenty-gallon jug of earth in which the young fir tree grows. The story celebrates lateral thinking, the power of pattern recognition, and the quiet modesty of true intelligence—Leroy never boasts, choosing instead to blend in with ordinary boys.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the story to find out how the boy wonder solves a case that has been troubling his father. (Introductory reading task with pre-reading activities: I. Solve the crossword puzzle with anagram clues. II. Match different meanings of 'case' with sentences.)

question_bank.stem: Solve the crossword puzzle given below with the clues in the form of anagrams.
Match the different meanings of 'case' in Column 2 with the correct usage in the sentences in Column 1.
question_bank.table:
Column 1|Column 2
1. Lalit said that he could not sing but that was not the case as he sang melodiously later.|(i) writing format
2. The lawyer was waiting for the case to be taken up in court.|(ii) container
3. My pencil case is a gift from my grandmother.|(iii) situation
4. The subject of a formal letter is written in title case.|(iv) matter

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following statements with suitable reasons. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Leroy's nickname was Encyclopedia and everyone called him by that name because ______________.
At the dinner table on Tuesday night, Chief Brown stared at his cream-of-mushroom soup as ______________.
Encyclopedia sat quietly at the dinner table because ______________.
Nolan put the will on the kitchen table because ______________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think Chief Brown would need Leroy's help to solve this case? If yes, why? If no, why not?
A fact is something that can be proven true or false. An opinion is a belief, feeling, or judgement. Identify which of the following statements are facts or opinions.

question_bank.stem: Encyclopedia's father was the Chief of Police.
Everyone thought that Chief Brown must be the smartest police chief in the country.
An encyclopedia is a book or set of books filled with facts from A to Z.
Leroy's friends said that he was like a library and computer rolled into one, and more user-friendly.
Two masked men held up the Diamond Mart on Sixth Avenue.
Nolan and Davenport had met while both were in prison in South Carolina.
Chief Brown's hunch was that Davenport and Nolan decided to hide the loot until things cooled down.
Nolan wrote a four-word code to tell Davenport where he had hidden the stolen jewellery.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the table by choosing the character traits of Leroy from the box. There is an extra word you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match textual evidence with character traits: cleverness / knowledgeable / keen listener / gentle / humble.
question_bank.table:
Textual Evidence|Trait
1. He read more books than anyone in Idaville, and he never forgot a fact.|knowledgeable
2. Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father. He didn't want to seem different from other boys.|__
3. Encyclopedia sat quietly. He knew his mother and father were discussing the case for his benefit.|__
4. Usually, he needed to ask only one question to solve a case before dessert.|__

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: Chief Brown would have liked to tell everyone about his only child. But who would believe him? Who would believe that the best detective alive was an eighth grader? So, he said nothing. Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father. He didn't want to seem different from other boys…

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. Chief Brown's dilemma can be called unique because____________.

What can be inferred about why Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father? A. He is shy and lacks confidence in his abilities to share his insights. B. He wants to avoid the jealousy others might feel about his skills. C. He desires to blend with the other boys to prevent seeming different. D. He is embarrassed about being smarter than most adults around him.

The parents choose not to call their son 'Encyclopedia'. Select the reason that is false. A. For them he is more than his unique abilities; he is their child, with a personal identity. B. Calling him by his real name reflects their affection and the desire to acknowledge him as an ordinary boy. C. They want him to be remembered as different from the others around.
What does the writer mean by the fact that Leroy was 'stuck with' the name Encyclopedia?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow. Extract 2: Chief Brown studied the four words: Nom Utes Sweden Hurts. He shook his head and passed the notebook to Mrs. Brown again. 'Can you figure it out?' 'Nom is a shortening of nominative…' She lifted her gaze to Encyclopedia and shook her head. 'I can't figure it out,' she confessed.

question_bank.stem: What does Chief Brown's reaction of shaking his head suggest about his understanding of the four words? A. He is confident that the words are a clue. B. He is confused, unable to make sense of the words. C. He believes the words are random and meaningless. D. He thinks the words are unrelated to the case.
Identify whether the given statement is true or false. Mrs. Brown explained the actual meanings of the individual words rather than decoding the hidden message behind them.
What does Mrs. Brown's inability to decode the words suggest about the message?

Complete the following sentence suitably. The purpose of Encyclopedia's reminder in the last line of the extract was to _________________. (refocus the discussion on the context of the crime / suggest that the coded message was meant for Davenport / imply that the message was likely a clue about the location of the stolen jewellery)
options: refocus the discussion on the context of the crime
suggest that the coded message was meant for Davenport
imply that the message was likely a clue about the location of the stolen jewellery

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Nolan and Davenport were very close. Support this statement with evidence from the text.
Why did Davenport disappear right after the jewellery hold-up? What might his plans have been if Nolan had not died?
What does Mrs. Brown's interest in the case tell us about her?
Explain why Chief Brown was proud of his son.
Why were Chief Brown's suspicions regarding Nolan and Davenport justified, even when there was a lack of concrete proof?
Analyse the role of the four-word coded message in the story.

Write a character sketch on Leroy with the help of the character traits table given in 'Let us discuss' section. You may begin it like this: Encyclopedia was a knowledgeable eighth grader who never forgot a fact that he read.

listening.transcript_text:
HOST: Hello everyone! In today's episode, we'll be discussing some simple but effective ways to improve our observation skills. Being observant isn't just about seeing, it's about truly noticing details in the world around us. Whether it's in daily life or in the workplace, observation skills can help us become more aware, make better decisions, and even improve memory.
First, slow down and take your time. Rushing through tasks often leads to missed details. When you take time to focus on what's in front of you, you're more likely to notice features you might otherwise overlook. When you pause and observe, the details become clearer.
Another effective method is to engage all your senses. Observation isn't just about seeing; it's also about hearing, smelling, touching, and even tasting. The more senses you engage, the more information you absorb.
A third tip is to ask questions. Curious people tend to be better observers because they actively try to understand what they're noticing. Asking questions forces your brain to think critically about what you're observing.
Next, take notes or make sketches. Writing down what you observe can help reinforce details in your memory. Even quick sketches of an object or place can strengthen your understanding of its features.
Finally, it's important to practise mindfulness. Mindfulness helps you stay present in the moment and fully engage with your surroundings. Start incorporating these techniques into your daily routine, and soon you'll be observing things you never noticed before!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a podcast about ways of improving observation skills. As you listen, mark the statements 1–4 as true or false.

question_bank.stem: Observation skills help improve your common sense.
Rushing through tasks helps you focus better and observe more.
Taking notes or making sketches helps with what you observe.
Incorporating tips on observation skills will soon lead to success.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will once again listen to the podcast. As you listen, complete the given sentences by filling in the blanks with one to three exact words you hear.

question_bank.stem: It is important to slow down and ___________________ to notice several aspects around you more clearly.
The more senses like hearing and smelling you use, the more ___________________ you absorb.
People who are ___________________ tend to be better observers because they seek to understand what they notice.
When you make a note of things, it helps reinforce the ________________ in your memory.
Practising mindfulness helps a person stay ___________________ with their surroundings.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the text loudly with the help of pronunciation guide given in brackets: jewellery (jool-ree), clerk (claak), dessert (dih-zuht). Now, practise reading the following words: January, sour, pizza, Wednesday, bowl, buffet, pronunciation.

Work in groups of five or six. Conduct a group discussion on the topic, 'The Interesting Aspects of a Mystery Story'. The moderator initiates the discussion, speakers take turns using polite expressions to agree or disagree, speakers may interrupt by raising their hands, the moderator ensures equal participation, and the moderator sums up and thanks members. Use the given phrases for turn-taking, agreement, and disagreement.

Now, as a member of the Art and Craft Club, write a report on an 'Inter-school Art Exhibition' organised by your school. Include the necessary details. (Sample report on inter-school football tournament is provided showing format: title with byline, paragraph 1: what/who/when/where, paragraphs 2-3: detailed description with sequence markers, paragraph 4: prize-giving ceremony and remarks.)
Match the phrasal verbs from the text given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. You may refer to a dictionary. Then fill in the blanks with the phrasal verbs.

question_bank.stem: Match phrasal verbs: put on / cooled down / turned up / make of / figure out / got away with — with their meanings: appeared/been discovered / solve a problem / became normal or calmed / wear / escaped without being punished / have an idea or understanding of something.
question_bank.table:
Phrasal Verb|Meaning
put on|(i) appeared, been discovered
cooled down|(ii) solve a problem
turned up|(iii) became normal or calmed
make of|(iv) wear (clothes, hat, etc.)
figure out|(v) escaped without being punished
got away with|(vi) have an idea or understanding of something

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with the phrasal verbs: A. Finally, some important evidence about the case has ___. B. After I ___, I understood that I had made a big mistake. C. I couldn't ___ why he was angry with me. D. He ___ his special coat and went to his friend's birthday party. E. Ravi forgets to do his homework but he ___ it as teacher didn't ask for it. F. I do not know what to ___ his behaviour.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks in the following sentences by choosing the correct word pairs from the box: week/weak, dying/dyeing, fore/four, peace/piece, break/brake.

question_bank.stem: After running for 50 kilometres last _________________, I felt _________________ for two days.
When the committee met yesterday, they brought _________________ local cases to the _________________.
While learning to drive, Rina used too much force on the _________________, causing the car to _________________ down.
He has composed a beautiful _________________ of music in order to bring _________________ to thousands of people.
She was busy _________________ her hair not caring about the _________________ flowers in her garden.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Leroy solved the mystery of the missing fifth word with his critical thinking abilities. Work in pairs to solve the puzzles given in the illustrations and infer what they say. (Visual word puzzles: e.g., 'TOP SECRET' = top secret; 'It stand It' = understand; 'AGED AGED AGED' = middle aged; 'Try uu GO' = try it out; '2 It' = too good; 'ONCE R' = once in a while; multiple 'sense' = common sense; '1 M I L L I O N R O A D S' = million roads/possibilities.)

Spoonerism is a mistake made when speaking where the first sounds of the words in a phrase are exchanged. Find the correct order for the following spoonerisms: 1. knowing sits 2. it's roaring with pain 3. plaster man 4. blarm wanket 5. tars and crucks 6. shake a tower 7. bead a rook 8. mardon me padam.

question_bank.stem: Find the correct (original) phrases for each spoonerism: 1. knowing sits 2. it's roaring with pain 3. plaster man 4. blarm wanket 5. tars and crucks 6. shake a tower 7. bead a rook 8. mardon me padam.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the sentences from the text and study the highlighted relative/subordinating conjunctions. Fill in the blanks with the words in the box (where/why/who/though/which/when/because/whose/if/how) to complete a paragraph about detective stories. There are two extra words you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Detective stories are exciting, keeping readers guessing (i) ____________ the real culprit is. They take place in settings (ii) ____________ clues are hidden carefully. (iii) ____________ the mystery seems tricky, a clever detective always finds answers. Readers often wonder (iv) ____________ criminals leave small but important clues. The challenge is to decide (v) ____________ suspect is guilty. (vi) ____________ the truth is revealed, everything makes sense. But one may wonder (vii) ____________ they could have solved it themselves. These stories are fun (viii) ____________ they make readers think till the very end.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Combine the following pairs of sentences using the word given in brackets. (Example given: 'We gave him the signal that he was waiting for.')

question_bank.stem: We went to a place. The incident occurred there. (where)
Teacher said she would retire soon. She is now nearly sixty. (who)
It was the day. Half the class was absent. (when)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the direct speech sentences from the text (interrogative sentences). Learn the rules for transforming direct to indirect (reported) speech: tense changes, pronoun changes, time and place changes, steps for Wh- questions and Yes/No questions. Now, follow these steps to transform the sentences given as examples from direct speech to indirect speech.

question_bank.stem: (iv) Mrs. Brown asked Chief Brown ________________________________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Rewrite the following sentences in Reported Speech.

question_bank.stem: 'Why did you stop talking to me?' asked Rohan. Rohan questioned __________.
'Did you enjoy the family gathering yesterday?' asked my aunt. My aunt asked ______.
'When will you visit us again?' asked my grandmother. My grandmother inquired ______.
Madavi said to Ravi, 'Will you come with me to meet our old teacher tomorrow?' Madavi asked Ravi ______.
My friend said, 'What gift are you planning to give your brother?' My friend inquired ______.
'Shall I carry these notebooks to the staffroom, Ma'am?' asked Veenu. Veenu asked her teacher ______.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the conversation between Aditi and Rahul. Complete the following paragraph by transforming the dialogue into indirect speech.

question_bank.stem: Aditi asked Rahul 1. ___. Rahul replied that he had noticed a man wearing a blue jacket drop it off. Aditi asked Rahul 2. ___. Rahul replied that it had been around 6.45 p.m. Aditi further inquired 3. ___. Rahul said that he had worn a hood, so he couldn't see his face.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Read the words and observe the images of optical illusions. Check if your answers match — these are optical illusions which may trick the eye and make you think that you can see two different things. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

Read the steps to play a language game — 'Name the Mystery Object'. Divide the class into teams. Each team chooses an object and prepares three hints. Teams take turns to name the object. Points: first hint = 20, second hint = 10, third hint = 5. One example provided with three hints about a blackboard.
Visit the library and look for an encyclopedia. What is it? Check how it is different from a dictionary. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
A thesaurus is a reference book or digital tool that provides a collection of words with similar meanings. Choose any five words and find out their alternative words from the thesaurus.
